title: Nick Ball gives away five inches to Leigh Wood, and that's rather the point
summary: Nick Ball is 5ft 2in, Leigh Wood 5ft 7in. For Ball, the height gap is not a problem to solve. It is the whole engine of how he fights.
body:
Nick Ball stands 5ft 2in. Leigh Wood stands 5ft 7in. In most sports a five-inch gap would settle an argument before it began. In Ball's case it powers the way he fights. This featherweight meeting is a study in what a small, relentless pressure fighter does to a taller, rangier opponent.

Ball: small, and always coming forward

Ball's 23-1-1 record splits the difference between pressure and craft. He has 13 knockouts at 57 per cent, but also 10 wins on the cards, so the volume he throws does not come at the expense of being able to box. At 29, with a 65-inch reach, he has spent his career closing the distance that taller men want to keep. It is the only way a fighter his size survives, and he has turned it into a weapon.

Wood: the taller veteran with a dramatic past

Wood, 37, brings 29-4 and a near-identical finishing profile, 17 knockouts at 59 per cent plus 12 decisions. The warning sign is in his recent five, which contains two knockout losses. He has been in thrillers, and more than once he has been caught. He is eight years older than Ball and gives up nothing in reach, standing five inches taller.

Range against pressure

The fight frames itself. If Wood can use those five inches of height to keep Ball at the end of his punches, his experience over 33 contests should tell. If Ball gets underneath that range and turns it into a phone-box fight, the taller man's recent habit of being stopped moves to the centre of the story.
